target.name: Corintis
target.founder: Remco van Erp, Sam Harrison, Elison Matioli

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $25M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $500M - $5.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 20.6x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Etched
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $5B

private.name: d-Matrix
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $2B

private.name: Substrate
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $1B+

private.name: Rain AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $500M

data.narrative:
Corintis is a Series A chip design company founded by Remco van Erp, Sam Harrison, and Elison Matioli, having raised $25M to date. The semiconductors sector comprises a comp universe of 3 public names (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private peers, with particular relevance to recent Series A entrants including Etched ($5.0B), d-Matrix ($2.0B), Substrate ($1.0B), and Rain AI ($500M).

Two valuation methods are applicable. Stage-progression modeling, using the $25M raised as a base proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical for Series A advancement, implies a $50M valuation. Peer-median analysis across four same-stage, same-sector comparables yields a $1.5B implied valuation, with the peer set ranging from $500M (Rain AI) to $5.0B (Etched). Public-comp anchoring via semiconductors P/S multiples (20.6x median across the three tickers) cannot be applied due to absence of target revenue data, standard for private companies.

Triangulation of the two applicable methods produces a valuation range of $42.5M (low) to $1.7B (high), with a median of $775M. The wide dispersion reflects the stage-progression method's conservative reliance on capital raised versus the peer-median's incorporation of recent market pricing for frontier semiconductor ventures. Key uncertainties include technology differentiation relative to the peer set, capital efficiency metrics, and near-term milestones that would justify positioning within this range.
